briefings.headline: Oil hits $100 a barrel as Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers rattle markets
briefings.body: Oil surged to $100 per barrel after Iran-backed Houthi rebels attacked two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea, disrupting a critical global shipping corridor. Simultaneously, President Trump replaced a set of expiring tariffs with a new round of trade levies, adding further pressure to markets already reacting to the supply shock.

sources.title: Oil surges to $100 per barrel. And, Trump imposes a new round of tariffs
Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May

briefings.why_it_matters: Rising oil prices and new tariffs could drive up costs for fuel, goods, and transportation, hitting consumers directly at the pump and in stores.

briefings.headline: West Virginia's paid relocation program is turning temporary transplants into permanent residents
briefings.body: West Virginia's Ascend program, launched during COVID to attract remote workers with cash incentives, is seeing participants choose to stay long-term. The initiative is one of dozens of similar relocation programs across the country aimed at giving economically struggling regions a boost. Early results suggest financial incentives can successfully shift where remote workers choose to put down roots.

sources.title: West Virginia paid digital nomads to move there during COVID. Many are staying

briefings.why_it_matters: Remote workers considering a move could find paid relocation programs a viable way to lower living costs while states competing for residents may expand similar incentive offers.

briefings.headline: Justice Dept. drops New York Times subpoenas after federal judge's sharp questioning
briefings.body: The Justice Department has withdrawn subpoenas demanding grand jury testimony and phone records from New York Times journalists. The decision came after a federal judge questioned the subpoenas sharply during a Thursday hearing. The move ends an immediate legal confrontation between federal prosecutors and the Times over compelled journalist cooperation with a grand jury investigation.

sources.title: Justice Dept. drops 'New York Times' subpoenas under pressure from judge

briefings.why_it_matters: Journalists at major outlets could face less pressure to hand over source information or testify before grand juries, at least in the near term.

briefings.headline: Musk Says He Is Unbothered by Public Criticism Amid Growing AI Concerns
briefings.body: Elon Musk, speaking to The Economist, said he does not care if people hate him and denied being a racist. His comments came as fears around artificial intelligence continue to mount. Musk, who has been a prominent voice on AI development, struck a defiant tone, describing the current moment with the phrase 'let's enjoy the ride' as scrutiny of both his views and the technology intensifies.

sources.title: 'Let's enjoy the ride' says Elon Musk, as AI fears mount

briefings.why_it_matters: As one of the most influential figures shaping AI development and public policy, Musk's attitudes and statements could influence how quickly guardrails on powerful technologies are pursued or abandoned.

briefings.headline: Schools tackle writing instruction as the next frontier in literacy reform
briefings.body: As reading instruction has gained renewed focus in recent years, educators are turning attention to a parallel gap: students are not being taught to write effectively. At Sand Creek Elementary in Highlands Ranch, Colorado, teachers are implementing structured writing lessons beginning in kindergarten and first grade, with early results showing measurable growth. Experts suggest writing has long been undertaught in American classrooms, leaving students underprepared for academic and professional demands.

sources.title: The next literacy challenge: Teaching students how to write

briefings.why_it_matters: Students who never receive explicit writing instruction could struggle to communicate clearly in higher education and the workforce, affecting their long-term economic and academic opportunities.

briefings.headline: Rayner Rules Out Rent Controls in England
briefings.body: Housing Secretary Angela Rayner has told the BBC that rent controls will not be introduced in England, arguing the Renters Rights Act is already having a significant impact on the housing market. The decision closes off a policy option that tenant advocates had pushed for as a tool to curb rising rents, signalling the government's approach will rely on existing tenancy reforms rather than direct price intervention.

sources.title: Angela Rayner rules out rent controls in England

briefings.why_it_matters: Renters facing rising housing costs could find their options for relief limited to protections already established under the Renters Rights Act, with no government cap on how much landlords can charge.

quick_hits.text: A BBC visit to Minab, Iran reveals ongoing grief nearly five months after an airstrike killed 120 children at a school.

source.title: 'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children

quick_hits.text: Google has been fined $1 billion by the EU under the Digital Markets Act, becoming the third tech giant penalized under the landmark law.

source.title: Google hit with $1 billion in fines as EU braces for Trump battle

quick_hits.text: The Justice Department withdrew subpoenas targeting New York Times reporters, according to the paper's lead newsroom lawyer David McCraw.

source.title: New York Times lawyer talks about DOJ withdrawing subpoenas sent to Times' reporters

quick_hits.text: A historic cyclospora outbreak is devastating Michigan, with infected residents reporting bathroom trips up to 20 times daily.

source.title: Using a bathroom 20 times a day - life in the middle of US cyclospora outbreak

quick_hits.text: The Trump administration eliminated SNAP-Ed, a federal nutrition education program, despite championing its Make America Healthy Again initiative.

source.title: The White House pushes healthy eating. It cut a landmark nutrition education program

voice_blocks.title: This Day
voice_blocks.text: 2025: Angara Airlines Flight 2311 crashes on approach to Tynda Airport, killing all 48 people on board.

closer.text: "Knowledge will forever govern ignorance; and a people who mean to be their own governors must arm themselves with the power which knowledge gives."
closer.attribution: James Madison, letter to W.T. Barry (1822)

source.title: James Madison, letter to W.T. Barry (1822)
